title: Stay away from energy drinks and energy bars if you want to be lean

transcript.text: Hello, this video is all you should stay away from energy drinks and energy bars, seriously.
I saw a video the other day, which got me kind of thinking about it, by Jamie Eason,
Who's like the top, you know, figure model for body space, figure competitor.
And she says that it's impossible to stay lean if you eat energy bars because of the chemicals.
I don't know how it works, but I guess if you eat anything with chemicals, the chemicals turn into fat.
So I guess this belly you have around you, it's made out of chemicals.
And then there's this other guy named Superman who's a figure competitor, or not a figure, bodybuilding competitor from body space.
And he says that all he drinks is water, and he's got like 6.7 body fat.
Now me, I've been working out, I work out four hours a day, and I've been working out for like years.
And I have a pretty clean diet.
I eat vegetables and fruit pretty much is all I eat.
I cook my own food.
Every couple of weeks I'll have an alcohol and candy binge, but I pretty much have a clean diet, and I work out four hours a day,
But I have 18% body fat.
I have a big ass belly.
Here, maybe we can film my belly.
I've got to be careful here, though.
Blippity, blibbity, blibbity, blibbity, you see?
And I was like, why?
And then I'm thinking it's from the energy drinks, because I'm pretty much addicted to energy drinks.
I was drinking Nox.
Even though they're sugar-free, I was drinking Nox.
And then now I'm drinking Enduro Rush, which is totally hardcore in your drink.
You're only supposed to drink like half of a bottle every time you work out.
And I kind of developed a dependency for him, like, because I have energy problems a lot for some reason.
And I'm thinking my energy problems are from the energy drinks.
I just have developed a dependency on the caffeine.
And, I mean, if you look at the ingredients on this Enduro Rush, it's got, like, a couple dozen, I guess, stuff that you've never even heard of.
You can't even pronounce, literally.
And, I mean, I think those drinks are really only good, including creatine, are really only good for, like, the, you know, once in a blue moon, if you want to, like, break your PR or something.
And it's really important to you to, like, be extra strong that day or whatever.
But you basically want to stay, you want to stay away from that stuff.
And I think, I think, so I'm going to get away from it.
Even, it's going to totally mess up with my energy, and I'm going to be, like, groggy the whole time.
But hopefully in a couple weeks, it'll cycle through me, and I'll be okay.
Plus, I'll save a lot of money.
Those knocks, those Enduro Rushes are four bucks each.
So, yeah, energy bars and energy drinks are basically, you know, it's processed food.
It's like eating, you know, potato chips and Doritos and McDonald's and microwave burritos and stuff, you know.
And soda pop, I'll throw the energy drinks and the energy bars in there, too.
You know, only drink, only eat energy bars if you're, like, an Enduro cyclist, you know.
An Enduro cyclist would never have a problem packing on fat with fat problems.
So, yeah, stay away from that stuff because you will not stay lean.
And, you know, I'm ready to be lean.
I want to be lean.
Like, I weigh 190 pounds.
And, I mean, if I lost all this fat, I'd probably look pretty fucking sexy.
I'd probably weigh, like, 180, and then I'd be able to do a lot more push-ups, you know.
Like, I was only able to do, like, 20 push-ups a day.
So, I think it screws with your energy.
And, overall, it screws with your energy.
It intoxicates you.
It's bad for you.
And it makes you fat.
So.
